<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The notes homepage should list the note with the most connections first.

Connections likely mean total degree: outgoing links plus incoming backlinks. I'll check for existing count logic and update list.html to sort by connection count.

A partial template could count outgoing wikilinks and incoming backlinks per note. Total connections would be the sum of both counts.

The current list uses `sort-featured-first.html` with title sort. The first item should specifically be the most connected note, while the remaining items may keep their existing order.

The entire list should be sorted by connection count descending. The most connected note will appear first, followed by the rest in descending order.</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The notes list currently uses `sort-featured-first`.

A reusable partial will compute each note's connection count by combining outgoing wikilinks and incoming backlinks. Another partial will sort the notes list so the most-connected item appears first.

The graph-data partial already computes all edges. A `connection-counts.html` partial will build a dictionary mapping each note to its total link count. This avoids scanning every note twice. The notes list will use that map to sort items.

A single `sort-by-connections.html` partial may handle both counting and sorting. Connection counts will sum distinct outgoing wikilinks and incoming backlinks. Sorting in Hugo requires building a custom slice from a dictionary map.

The concrete Hugo pattern merges counts into a dictionary keyed by relative permalink. It then appends each note and its count into a slice, sorts that slice descending by count, and iterates over the results using `.page`.

Counting connections individually for every note would be too slow. The graph-data edges will instead feed a single pass that increments counts for both source and target nodes. A wikilink from A to B contributes one outgoing link for A and one incoming link for B.

Undirected degree is the right metric for hub nodes. Incrementing dictionary values directly in Hugo is awkward, so a scratch variable will accumulate the counts instead.

The scratch variable will use `Add` to increment counts for both source and target nodes. Hugo automatically initializes missing keys to zero, so no manual setup is needed.

Ties in connection counts need a secondary sort key. Title order could break stability, so a combined sort key or chained sort is being considered.

A padded sort key will resolve ties by combining the negative connection count with the title. This avoids relying on Hugo's unstable multi-key sorting.

The sort key will pad the negative connection count with the title, so ties sort alphabetically. The existing `sort-featured-first` logic will be replaced entirely by `sort-by-connections`, ensuring the most-connected note always leads regardless of the featured flag. Other templates that list notes, such as `notes/single.html`, will also be checked for updates.

The `sort-by-connections` partial only needs the `Pages` slice passed in its dictionary. It does not require the page context because `graph-data` delegates to `pages.html`, which reads `site.Pages` directly.

The sort partial will receive the section page context as `Context`. It will pass that context to `graph-data.html` so the edge scan runs against the correct page scope.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
"Active Knowledge Curation" appears first on the notes homepage rather than "Maps of Content".</details></think>

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: curl -s http://localhost:1314/notes/ | python3 -c "
import re, sys
html = sys.stdin.read()
cards = re.findall(r'<article class=\"notes-card[^\"]*\".*?</article>', html, re.S)
for i, c in enumerate(cards[:10]):
    m = re.search(r'<h2[^>]*>.*?<a[^>]*>([^<]+)</a>', c, re.S)
    if m: print(f'{i+1}. {m.group(1)}')
"</summary>

```bash
curl -s http://localhost:1314/notes/ | python3 -c "
import re, sys
html = sys.stdin.read()
cards = re.findall(r'<article class=\"notes-card[^\"]*\".*?</article>', html, re.S)
for i, c in enumerate(cards[:10]):
    m = re.search(r'<h2[^>]*>.*?<a[^>]*>([^<]+)</a>', c, re.S)
    if m: print(f'{i+1}. {m.group(1)}')
"
```

```
1. Active Knowledge Curation
2. Maps of Content
3. Advantages of Digital Gardens
4. Building a Second Brain
5. Analog Capture Tools
6. Capture
7. Associative Linking
8. Evergreen Notes
9. Anti-Fragile Systems
10. Atomic Design for Notes

```

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="run_terminal_command_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **run_terminal_command_v2** • Run command: cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 << 'PY'
import os, re, glob

notes_dir = "content/english/notes"
files = [f for f in glob.glob(f"{notes_dir}/*.md") if not f.endswith("_index.md")]

def targets_for(path, title):
    base = os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(path))[0]
  # read aliases from frontmatter - simplified
    with open(path) as f:
        content = f.read()
    aliases = []
    if content.startswith("---"):
        fm, body = content.split("---", 2)[1:]
        body = content.split("---", 2)[2]
        for line in fm.splitlines():
            if line.strip().startswith("aliases:"):
                pass  # skip complex
    else:
        body = content
    with open(path) as f:
        raw = f.read()
    # extract title from frontmatter
    title_match = re.search(r'^title:\s*["\']?(.+?)["\']?\s*$', raw, re.M)
    t = title_match.group(1) if title_match else base
    needles = {t, base}
    alias_match = re.search(r'^aliases:\s*\[(.*?)\]', raw, re.M)
    if alias_match:
        for a in re.findall(r'["\']([^"\']+)["\']', alias_match.group(1)):
            needles.add(a)
    return needles, raw

note_data = {}
for path in files:
    needles, raw = targets_for(path, None)
    slug = os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(path))[0]
    note_data[slug] = {"needles": needles, "raw": raw, "path": path}

# build edges like hugo
edges = []
slugs = list(note_data.keys())
for from_slug in slugs:
    raw = note_data[from_slug]["raw"]
    for to_slug in slugs:
        if from_slug == to_slug:
            continue
        for needle in note_data[to_slug]["needles"]:
            if f"[[{needle}]]" in raw or re.search(rf'\[\[{re.escape(needle)}\|[^\]]+\]\]', raw):
                edges.append((from_slug, to_slug))
                break

counts = {s: 0 for s in slugs}
for s, t in edges:
    counts[s] += 1
    counts[t] += 1

for slug, c in sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda x: (-x[1], x[0]))[:15]:
    with open(note_data[slug]["path"]) as f:
        title = re.search(r'^title:\s*["\']?(.+?)["\']?\s*$', f.read(), re.M).group(1)
    print(f"{c:3d}  {title}")
PY</summary>

```bash
cd /Users/jonathanrapusas/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com && python3 << 'PY'
import os, re, glob

notes_dir = "content/english/notes"
files = [f for f in glob.glob(f"{notes_dir}/*.md") if not f.endswith("_index.md")]

def targets_for(path, title):
    base = os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(path))[0]
  # read aliases from frontmatter - simplified
    with open(path) as f:
        content = f.read()
    aliases = []
    if content.startswith("---"):
        fm, body = content.split("---", 2)[1:]
        body = content.split("---", 2)[2]
        for line in fm.splitlines():
            if line.strip().startswith("aliases:"):
                pass  # skip complex
    else:
        body = content
    with open(path) as f:
        raw = f.read()
    # extract title from frontmatter
    title_match = re.search(r'^title:\s*["\']?(.+?)["\']?\s*$', raw, re.M)
    t = title_match.group(1) if title_match else base
    needles = {t, base}
    alias_match = re.search(r'^aliases:\s*\[(.*?)\]', raw, re.M)
    if alias_match:
        for a in re.findall(r'["\']([^"\']+)["\']', alias_match.group(1)):
            needles.add(a)
    return needles, raw

note_data = {}
for path in files:
    needles, raw = targets_for(path, None)
    slug = os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(path))[0]
    note_data[slug] = {"needles": needles, "raw": raw, "path": path}

# build edges like hugo
edges = []
slugs = list(note_data.keys())
for from_slug in slugs:
    raw = note_data[from_slug]["raw"]
    for to_slug in slugs:
        if from_slug == to_slug:
            continue
        for needle in note_data[to_slug]["needles"]:
            if f"[[{needle}]]" in raw or re.search(rf'\[\[{re.escape(needle)}\|[^\]]+\]\]', raw):
                edges.append((from_slug, to_slug))
                break

counts = {s: 0 for s in slugs}
for s, t in edges:
    counts[s] += 1
    counts[t] += 1

for slug, c in sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda x: (-x[1], x[0]))[:15]:
    with open(note_data[slug]["path"]) as f:
        title = re.search(r'^title:\s*["\']?(.+?)["\']?\s*$', f.read(), re.M).group(1)
    print(f"{c:3d}  {title}")
PY
```

_**Agent (auto-low)**_

The notes homepage now sorts by **wikilink connections** — each note’s degree in the graph (incoming + outgoing links). The most connected note appears first.

**Changes:**
1. Added `layouts/_partials/notes/sort-by-connections.html` — builds connection counts from the same graph edge data used by the graph view, then sorts descending by count (title as tiebreaker).
2. Updated `layouts/notes/list.html` — replaced the featured-first/title sort with the new connection-based sort.

Right now **Active Knowledge Curation** (49 connections) is first, followed by **Maps of Content** (48). That order updates automatically as you add or remove `[[wikilinks]]`.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Line 263 already contains `* 2`.

The existing `* 2` may be insufficient for true 200% zoom. The `Math.min(...)` cap at 1.8 could suppress the fit scale for dense graphs. The user might also want this applied only to the full-page notes graph.

A default zoom scale of 2.0 would cleanly reach 200% zoom. The 1.8 cap should be raised since the multiplier already doubles the value. The `* 2` on line 263 may have been added in a prior edit.

I will add a `defaultZoom = 2` constant and apply it in `fitToView` as `k = Math.max(k, 0.25) * defaultZoom`. This targets the main notes graph page, not local graphs.

The existing `* 2` may be too weak. For roughly seventy nodes the fit scale is only about 0.15, so doubling it yields 0.3. That is still too small for a readable graph. A stronger default zoom is needed.

The existing `* 2` may be ineffective if fit-to-view recalculates afterward and overrides the zoom. Duplicate `boot()` calls or ResizeObserver resets could be wiping the scale.

The user might mean "zoom in by 200%" as a 3× total increase rather than a 2× multiplier. The code may also lack the existing `* 2` entirely, so I will add a clear `DEFAULT_ZOOM = 2` constant.</details></think>

Local graphs will stay at 1x zoom. Only the page graph panel gets the 2x multiplier.

The existing `* 2` multiplier applies to all graphs, so it may not be the right place for a page-specific zoom. I will introduce a `defaultZoom` variable set to 2 for page graphs and 1 for local graphs. The zoom will then be applied inside `fitToView` as `k = Math.max(k, 0.25) * defaultZoom`. I am also considering whether the user actually needs 4x total zoom rather than 2x.

I ruled out hardcoding zoom to 2.0 because large graphs would overflow. I also considered whether the user meant the zoom-in button should double each click. I will keep the 2x default zoom inside fitToView and replace the magic number with a named constant.

Even with the 2x multiplier, a dense page graph may still render at roughly 0.7x scale. I am now considering forcing the page graph to a fixed minimum zoom of 2.0, though that would heavily crop large layouts.

The 2x multiplier is already present in the code. This appears to be new work, so the fix may already exist. A caching issue is unlikely.

The default zoom will also be applied in the reset handler. I will set `defaultZoom` early in `initGraph` based on the page graph class, then multiply the final scale by it.

Removing the 1.8 cap does not help large graphs, since fitK is already below that limit. The real issue is that fitK itself is small for wide layouts, so multiplying by 2 still yields a modest zoom. True 200% zoom may require a fixed minimum scale of 1.0 or 2.0.</details></think>
